title: Agentic Public Benefits Platform — System Design
description: Design the platform for government benefits / eligibility agents — intake, evidence gathering, eligibility determination, and renewals — where a decision affects a citizen's rights and must be contestable. It adds a gate dimension the rest of the series doesn't have: due process. The decision record must be legible to the affected person and an independent reviewer, with an appeal path and explainability-for-appeal built in, not just a compliance log. It builds on Agentic Platform Foundations and adds the things that make this domain distinct: grounding in effective-dated, jurisdiction-tagged eligibility rules, a deterministic rule engine the model cannot override, a caseworker who decides every adverse outcome, and population-scale fairness monitoring.

nodes.label: Applicant / Claimant
nodes.description: The person applying for benefits; has a right to understand and contest the decision.

nodes.label: Caseworker
nodes.description: Public servant who reviews and decides adverse outcomes; the accountable decision-maker.

nodes.label: Appeals Reviewer
nodes.description: Independent reviewer who hears an appeal using the decision record.

nodes.label: Application / Evidence
nodes.description: The benefits application and supporting documents submitted by the applicant.

nodes.label: Benefits API
nodes.description: Authenticates the application, resolves scope, routes to the determination pipeline.

nodes.label: Determination Pipeline
nodes.description: Deterministic DAG: gather evidence -> apply rules -> explain -> caseworker decision.

nodes.label: Intake / Explanation Agent
nodes.description: LLM in a bounded node: extracts evidence and drafts a plain-language explanation. It does not decide.

nodes.label: Inference Backend
nodes.description: Shared LLM serving tier (from the Foundations platform).

nodes.label: Eligibility Rule Index
nodes.description: Grounding corpus: statute and eligibility rules, effective-dated and jurisdiction-tagged.

nodes.label: Deterministic Rule Engine
nodes.description: Applies eligibility rules to evidence to produce the determination. The model cannot override it.

nodes.label: Applicant / Case Record
nodes.description: The applicant's case data and prior history; the read and action surface (scoped).

nodes.label: Determination Gate
nodes.description: Routes adverse determinations to a caseworker; the agent never finalizes an adverse decision.

nodes.label: Adverse-Review Queue
nodes.description: Holds adverse-pending determinations with priority, SLA aging, retry, and deadline escalation; levels surge load so 'human in the loop' never becomes failure-by-delay.

nodes.label: Notice & Evidence Service
nodes.description: Sends evidence-needed requests and legible notices to the applicant in their language/format, starts the statutory appeal clock on delivery, and exposes the appeal packet.

nodes.label: Determination Record
nodes.description: The decision plus the rules applied, evidence used, and plain-language reasons — built for appeal.

nodes.label: Appeal Path
nodes.description: Lets an applicant contest a decision; routes the determination record to an independent reviewer.

nodes.label: Population Fairness Monitor
nodes.description: Tracks determination rates and consistency across groups and caseworkers at population scale.

nodes.label: Privacy & Policy
nodes.description: PII scoping, tenant isolation, and screening of untrusted application text.

nodes.label: Identity & Token Broker
nodes.description: Scoped, delegated access; binds an adverse decision to an accountable caseworker.

nodes.label: Audit Record
nodes.description: Append-only record: evidence + versions, rules applied, explanation, decision, appeals.

nodes.label: Observability & Eval
nodes.description: Trajectories, determination-rate dashboards, correctness and contestability eval.

functional: Intake applications and extract supporting evidence.
Determine eligibility by applying the correct statute/rules for the applicant's date and jurisdiction.
Produce a plain-language explanation of the decision and the rules and evidence behind it.
Route every adverse determination to an accountable caseworker.
Provide an appeal path: let the applicant contest, with the record routed to an independent reviewer.
nonFunctional: Contestability / due process: every decision is explainable to the applicant and reviewable on appeal.
A deterministic rule engine produces the determination; the model assists and explains but cannot override it.
Adverse decisions are decided by an accountable human, never finalized by the agent.
Consistency and fairness across applicants, groups, and caseworkers at population scale.
PII privacy and an auditable, legible determination record.

capacity.label: Applications
capacity.note: Steady-state intake; seasonal enrollment and annual recertification batches drive multi-x surges that a queue must level.

capacity.label: Evidence per application
capacity.note: Pay stubs, IDs, and official records the intake node extracts into structured evidence.

capacity.label: Adverse rate
capacity.note: Roughly a quarter of determinations route to a caseworker; these set the human-review backlog.

capacity.label: Caseworker throughput
capacity.note: Bounds adverse-pending drain rate; assignment, escalation, and SLA targets govern the queue.

capacity.label: Appeal rate
capacity.note: Appeals run against statutory deadlines; the appeal window starts at notice delivery, not at decision.

capacity.label: Re-determination load
capacity.note: A rule-threshold or effective-date change can trigger batch re-determination across affected open cases.

capacity.label: Adverse decisions
capacity.note: A caseworker decides every adverse determination; the agent never finalizes a denial.

capacity.label: Determination
capacity.note: Rules decide eligibility; the model extracts evidence and explains but cannot override the engine.

capacity.label: Failure behavior
capacity.note: If rule lookup, extraction, or reviewer assignment is delayed, the case holds in evidence_needed / adverse_pending rather than defaulting to a denial.

api.description: Run a determination: extract evidence, apply rules, and draft an explanation for caseworker review — the agent does not finalize an adverse outcome. Idempotent on the idempotency key so a retried submission never creates a second case.
Read the current workflow status, the eligibility/review outcome, the rules applied, and the applicant-facing plain-language reasons for a determination.

Supply supplemental or corrected evidence; re-runs extraction and the rule engine against the same pinned rule package. The re-evaluation can clear the case to determination_ready, route it to adverse_pending for a caseworker, or stay in evidence_needed if still incomplete.

A caseworker finalizes an adverse-pending determination; the decision is bound to an accountable principal. approve / deny accept the engine result; override_to_eligible overrides a denial with a recorded rationale (flagged for supervisor review); request_more_evidence sends the case back to the applicant for supplemental evidence.
Deliver the legible notice to the applicant in their language/format and start the statutory appeal-deadline clock.
An applicant contests a decision before the deadline; the determination record is routed to an independent reviewer.
An independent reviewer disposes of an appeal from the record; the reviewer must not be the original decider. The disposition becomes the determination's new outcome.
Export the appeal packet: the rules applied (with versions), evidence used, explanation, and full status history.

dataModel.name: determinations
dataModel.note: An eligibility determination through its full lifecycle, pinned to the rule package that decided it. Two orthogonal axes: status is workflow state (submitted -> evidence_needed -> determination_ready -> adverse_pending -> finalized -> noticed -> appealed -> reviewer_decided), and outcome is the eligibility/review result (eligible/denied at finalize; upheld/reversed/remanded after a reviewer disposition). noticed/appealed/reviewer_decided are lifecycle states of a determination whose outcome stays eligible or denied until a reviewer changes it.

dataModel.name: status_history
dataModel.note: Append-only state transitions — reconstructs the lifecycle for appeal and SLA tracking.

dataModel.name: evidence_items
dataModel.note: Structured evidence with provenance, confidence, and human corrections.

dataModel.name: explanations
dataModel.note: Plain-language reasons tied to rules and evidence (built for appeal).

dataModel.name: caseworker_decisions
dataModel.note: The accountable human decision on an adverse-pending determination.

dataModel.name: notices_appeals
dataModel.note: Notice delivery and appeal metadata — preserves due-process deadlines.

technologyChoices.concern: Benefits API edge, applicant access, and admission control
tradeoff: A dedicated API edge gives one place for applicant authentication, caseworker access, WAF rules, rate limits, and surge admission control.
Keeping access checks only inside application handlers is simpler early, but makes it harder to prove that every decision path is scoped the same way.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed gateway with WAF and quota controls removes most custom edge throttling and request-screening code.

technologyChoices.concern: Application document intake, OCR, and evidence extraction
tradeoff: Specialized OCR/document services improve extraction quality and support human correction loops, but they create another provenance boundary to audit.
Self-hosted OCR keeps raw applicant documents inside the agency boundary, but needs more work for layout, handwriting, and accessibility edge cases.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A document pipeline with malware scanning and extraction provenance removes the need for each downstream rule to inspect raw uploads directly.

technologyChoices.concern: Effective-dated rule corpus and jurisdiction index
tradeoff: A searchable rule corpus helps the agent ground explanations and lets reviewers inspect source authority, but the rule package still needs governed approval before use.
A relational package registry keeps effective dates, jurisdiction tags, and rollback metadata precise; search indexes should remain derived views.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A versioned rule registry removes ad-hoc code comments and wiki pages as the source of truth for which statute version applied.

technologyChoices.concern: Eligibility rule engine and package governance
tradeoff: DMN decision tables are legible to policy and legal reviewers and map cleanly to statute, but awkward for highly computed conditions.
Policy-as-code is expressive and unit-testable but needs a stronger approval workflow so legal meaning does not hide in application code.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A governed, effective-dated rule package removes hand-coded eligibility branches scattered across services.

technologyChoices.concern: Deterministic workflow orchestration and adverse-review queue
tradeoff: A durable workflow engine makes evidence waits, retries, appeal-clock deadlines, and escalation timers explicit.
A plain queue is cheaper and easier to reason about for one backlog, but pushes SLA aging and reassignment rules into application code.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Workflow timers and queue visibility remove many cron sweeps for stale adverse-pending determinations.

technologyChoices.concern: Source-of-truth determination record and status history
tradeoff: A relational store fits determinations, status transitions, evidence references, and appeal joins that reviewers must inspect.
A globally distributed database helps multi-region public programs but raises cost and schema-governance complexity.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A durable state model with explicit workflow status and eligibility outcome removes ambiguous lifecycle flags in downstream services.

technologyChoices.concern: Notice generation, delivery, localization, and appeal-clock evidence
tradeoff: A dedicated notice service centralizes language, accessibility, delivery proof, and statutory appeal-clock start time.
Letting the determination pipeline send notices directly is simpler but scatters due-process timing and template governance.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Centralized notice delivery removes per-program bespoke notification code and one-off appeal-deadline calculations.

technologyChoices.concern: Appeal packet storage, export, and reviewer routing
tradeoff: An exportable appeal packet makes the record portable to an independent reviewer, but requires strict redaction and retention controls.
Keeping appeal review in the core case system reduces integration work but can weaken reviewer independence if access and routing are not isolated.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A packaged appeal record removes manual caseworker PDF assembly as the path to contest a decision.

technologyChoices.concern: Identity delegation, accountable decisions, and authorization policy
tradeoff: A standards-based identity broker with delegated act claims binds every adverse decision to an accountable caseworker without impersonation.
Fine-grained authorization policy is heavier than role checks, but it is easier to audit when reviewers need to prove who could see or change a record.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Delegated tokens and policy checks remove shared service accounts from the adverse-decision path.

technologyChoices.concern: PII privacy, prompt-injection screening, secrets, and encryption
tradeoff: Separating secrets, PII classification, and untrusted text screening makes the rule engine harder to influence through applicant-supplied documents.
Deep inspection can add latency and false positives, so evidence-needed states must be explicit rather than silently dropping records.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Central PII and secret controls remove bespoke redaction and key-handling code from each pipeline node.

technologyChoices.concern: LLM serving, model gateway, and prompt/evidence cache
tradeoff: A model gateway centralizes model choice, prompt templates, redaction, caching, and token budgets while keeping the model outside the decision boundary.
Self-hosted inference gives data-control and latency options, but agency teams must own capacity planning and model patching.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A gateway with redaction and prompt-versioning removes per-agent direct model calls that are difficult to audit.

technologyChoices.concern: Immutable audit log, WORM retention, and provenance trail
tradeoff: Append-only audit makes evidence, rule version, explanation, notice delivery, and appeal events reconstructable years later.
WORM retention protects the record but increases the cost of correcting mistakes, so redaction and retention classes must be designed up front.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A tamper-evident audit stream removes spreadsheet exports as the compliance evidence of record.

technologyChoices.concern: Fairness analytics, protected-attribute governance, and cohort dashboards
tradeoff: A governed analytics warehouse keeps protected attributes partitioned off the determination path and supports matched-cohort analysis.
Disparity findings still require statistical and policy judgment; a dashboard should surface review, not declare legality.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Matched-cohort analytics make raw aggregate-rate dashboards insufficient as the only fairness signal.

technologyChoices.concern: Observability, trajectory evaluation, and due-process SLOs
tradeoff: Operational telemetry catches queue aging, notice-delivery failures, and caseworker backlog before they become due-process failures.
Trajectory evaluation measures extraction quality, explanation quality, and contestability, not just model-answer accuracy.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Shared traces and eval datasets remove one-off incident spreadsheets for reconstructing why a determination stalled.

patterns.name: Workflow vs agent (deterministic pipeline vs probabilistic loop)
patterns.what: An authored DAG (evidence -> rules -> explain -> decide), not an autonomous loop that could deny opaquely.
patterns.whenToUse: Where a decision affects a citizen's rights.

patterns.name: Human-in-the-loop approval (gate)
patterns.what: A caseworker decides every adverse determination; the agent never finalizes a denial.

patterns.name: Bounded autonomy (agent + deterministic gate)
patterns.what: The agent extracts and explains freely but a rule engine and caseworker gate fence the decision.
patterns.whenToUse: Assist-and-explain autonomy with a human-decision boundary.

patterns.name: Memory: short-term vs long-term (semantic/episodic/procedural)
patterns.what: Ground in effective-dated, jurisdiction-tagged eligibility rules and the applicant record.
patterns.whenToUse: Applying the right rule for the right applicant.

patterns.name: LLM-as-judge / trajectory evaluation
patterns.what: Evaluate determination correctness and explanation quality, and audit consistency across applicants.

patterns.name: Delegation not impersonation (OAuth token exchange, act claim)
patterns.what: Scoped access; an adverse decision is bound to an accountable caseworker principal.
patterns.whenToUse: Accessing the case record and recording decisions.

patterns.name: Prompt-injection defense (CaMeL / Dual-LLM / Plan-then-Execute)
patterns.what: Treat application free-text as untrusted; partition from rule-engine and record authority.

steps.title: 1. Naive: An Agent That Decides Eligibility (the baseline)
description: Start with the efficiency pitch: an agent reads an application and decides eligibility — approving or denying — from the model.

For a government benefit this is a due-process failure. A denial affects a person's rights, so it must be explainable and contestable; an opaque, model-made decision the applicant can't understand or challenge is exactly what administrative law forbids. Concretely, in this naive design the applicant cannot see the rule version that decided their case, correct the evidence that was used, identify an accountable decision-maker, or appeal from a complete record — and nothing guarantees the right rule version was even applied or checks that similar applicants get consistent outcomes.
The rest of the interview turns this into a deterministic, caseworker-decided, contestable determination with an appeal path.

view.caption: An agent decides eligibility opaquely from the model — the due-process-failing baseline.

steps.decisionPrompt: Why is an unexplainable automated denial unacceptable for a public benefit specifically?
patterns: Workflow vs agent (deterministic pipeline vs probabilistic loop)
whyNow: The naive opaque denial makes the contestability and explainability requirements concrete.

recap.after: An agent can produce an eligibility decision.
recap.newRisk: The decision is opaque, possibly applies the wrong rule, has no accountable human, and can't be contested.

traps.trap: Letting the model decide eligibility because it's faster.
traps.why: A benefits decision must be explainable and contestable; an opaque model decision denies due process.
traps.instead: Decide with a deterministic rule engine, have a caseworker own adverse outcomes, and build an appeal path.

steps.title: 2. Ground in Effective-Dated, Jurisdiction-Tagged Rules
description: The grounding corpus is the law: statute and eligibility rules, versioned by effective date and tagged by jurisdiction, plus the applicant's record. A determination must apply the rule that was in force for this applicant's date and place — not a stale or wrong-jurisdiction version.

A rule package is legally governed data, not just indexed text: each one carries its source authority and approval provenance, so a determination can name not only which rule it applied but who enacted it and when it took effect (step 3 details package versioning, golden-case testing, and rollback). Rule changes are governed too — a new package is approved, effective-date-tested, and rolled out before use, and each determination records the exact package version it applied. Applications are treated as untrusted input, and the applicant record is accessed under scope.

view.caption: Determinations apply the eligibility rule in force for the applicant's date and jurisdiction, against their record.

steps.decisionPrompt: Why isn't 'the current rulebook' enough to decide a determination correctly?

concepts.definition: Eligibility rules versioned by when and where they apply, so a determination uses the rule actually in force for the applicant.
concepts.whyItMatters: Applying the wrong rule version or jurisdiction produces a wrong, and challengeable, decision.
concepts.example: An application from last quarter is decided under that quarter's income thresholds, not today's.

whyNow: Correct, versioned grounding is the basis for a defensible, contestable decision.

recap.after: Determinations apply the correct rule version and jurisdiction.
recap.newRisk: Grounding alone doesn't make the decision deterministic or auditable — the rules must be applied by an engine, not the model.

steps.title: 3. A Deterministic Rule Engine (the Model Doesn't Decide)
description: The determination itself is made by a deterministic rule engine applied to the gathered evidence — not by the model. The LLM is confined to bounded nodes: extracting evidence from the application and drafting a plain-language explanation. It assists and explains; it does not decide.
This keeps eligibility decisions reproducible and auditable: the same evidence and rules always yield the same outcome, and the rule trace is part of the record.

view.caption: A deterministic rule engine makes the determination; the model only extracts evidence and explains — it cannot override the rules.

steps.decisionPrompt: What does the model do here, and why must the decision itself be deterministic?
patterns: Workflow vs agent (deterministic pipeline vs probabilistic loop)

options.name: Policy-as-code rules
options.summary: Eligibility encoded as versioned code modules.
pros: Full expressiveness and unit-testable against golden cases.
Versioned and reviewable in normal code review.
cons: Less legible to legal/policy reviewers than a decision table.
Rule changes need an engineering deploy.

options.name: Decision tables (DMN)
options.summary: Eligibility as DMN decision tables policy staff can read and review.
pros: Transparent and legally reviewable; maps cleanly to statute.
Effective-dated rows are easy to audit.
cons: Awkward for rules with complex computed conditions.
Still needs a governed deploy and golden-case tests.

options.name: Custom rules service
options.summary: A bespoke rules engine tuned to the program.
cons: Highest build and maintenance cost; bespoke audit tooling.
Hardest to keep transparent for appeal.

concepts.definition: The LLM extracts evidence and explains in plain language; a deterministic engine applies the rules to reach the determination.
concepts.whyItMatters: A rights-affecting decision must be reproducible and auditable, which a probabilistic model can't guarantee.
concepts.example: The agent reads pay stubs into structured income; the engine compares it to the threshold.

deepDives.title: Rule governance and surge leveling
points: Rule packages are versioned, effective-dated, and pinned to each determination so a later rule change never silently alters a past decision.
A new package is tested against golden determinations and legally reviewed before rollout; rollback restores the prior package.

Open-enrollment and recertification surges are absorbed by queueing with admission control: the Pipeline levels intake against finite extraction/rule-engine throughput, and the DecisionGate owns a separate adverse-review queue (priority, SLA aging, retry, deadline escalation) so spikes don't drop or mis-prioritize cases.
A rule-threshold change can enqueue a bounded batch re-determination across affected open cases rather than re-running everything at once.

whyNow: A deterministic decision is itself a due-process safeguard; it precedes the human gate.

recap.after: A deterministic engine decides; the model only extracts and explains.
recap.newRisk: An adverse determination still needs an accountable human to own it.

steps.title: 4. The Gate: Caseworker Decides, Decision Is Contestable
description: This is the crux, and the new gate dimension for the series: contestability. The deterministic engine produces a determination, but a caseworker decides every adverse outcome — the agent never finalizes a denial — and identity binds the decision to an accountable public servant.

Crucially, the decision ships with what's needed to contest it: the rules applied, the evidence used, and a plain-language explanation, all designed for the applicant and an independent reviewer to understand. Due process isn't an add-on; the gate produces a contestable decision by construction.

view.caption: Adverse determinations enter a priority/SLA review queue; a caseworker decides each one, and the decision ships with rules, evidence, and a plain-language explanation for contest.

steps.decisionPrompt: What must accompany an adverse decision so the applicant can actually contest it?

options.name: Queued adverse-pending review with SLA
options.summary: Adverse determinations enter a work queue; caseworkers draw from it under priority and SLA aging.
pros: Absorbs surge without dropping cases; deadline-driven prioritization is explicit.
Backlog and aging are measurable, so failure-by-delay is visible and governable.
cons: Adds queue infrastructure and SLA tracking.
An applicant waits in adverse_pending until a worker is assigned.

options.name: Synchronous blocking review
options.summary: The determination request blocks until a caseworker decides the adverse outcome inline.
pros: Simplest model; no separate queue or backlog state to manage.
Immediate decision for the applicant when staff are available.
cons: Collapses under surge — there is no buffer between intake spikes and finite caseworker throughput.
Couples request latency to staffing and offers no deadline-aware prioritization.

concepts.definition: A decision delivered with the rules, evidence, and plain-language reasons an applicant and reviewer need to challenge it.
concepts.whyItMatters: Due process requires that the affected person can understand and appeal the decision, not just receive it.
concepts.example: A denial states the income rule, the figure used, the source document, and how to appeal.

whyNow: The contestable-decision gate is what makes automated eligibility lawful and just.

deepDives.title: Caseworker queue mechanics
points: Adverse-pending determinations enter the adverse-review queue with assignment rules, priority, SLA targets, retry, and escalation for cases nearing a statutory deadline.
Caseworker actions are approve, deny, override_to_eligible, or request_more_evidence; an override_to_eligible is flagged for supervisor review and feeds rule-correction signals.
Workload and aging metrics surface backlog so 'human in the loop' never becomes failure-by-delay.
The decision is bound to the caseworker's identity so accountability is unambiguous.

recap.before: An adverse decision could be opaque and final.
recap.after: A caseworker owns adverse outcomes, and each decision is delivered ready to contest.
recap.newRisk: The contest path and the record's legibility must actually hold up on appeal.

traps.trap: Logging the decision for compliance but not for the applicant.
traps.why: A compliance log isn't a due-process record; the affected person must be able to understand and challenge it.
traps.instead: Make the determination record legible to the applicant and an independent reviewer.

steps.title: 5. Audit-for-Appeal & the Appeal Path
description: The determination record is built for appeal, not just for compliance. It maps the decision to the rules applied (with versions), the evidence used, and the plain-language explanation, so an applicant can contest and an independent reviewer can re-examine it.

A notice service owns the applicant-facing loop: it sends evidence-needed requests, delivers the legible notice in the applicant's language and accessible format, starts the statutory appeal clock on delivery, and exposes the exportable appeal packet. An appeal path then lets the applicant challenge the decision, routing the record to a reviewer who is not the original decider. Throughout, PII is scoped and application text is partitioned from the rule engine and record, so an injected document can never alter a determination.

view.caption: A notice service delivers the legible notice (starting the appeal clock) and the appeal packet; the appeal path routes the record to an independent reviewer.

steps.decisionPrompt: What's the difference between a compliance audit log and an audit-for-appeal record?
patterns: Delegation not impersonation (OAuth token exchange, act claim)

options.name: Materialized appeal packet
options.summary: Build and store the full appeal packet (rules, evidence, explanation, status history) at finalize time.
pros: Reviewers get an immediate, self-contained record; no reconstruction.
The exact record shown on appeal is frozen and auditable.
cons: Storage and write cost per determination, even for cases never appealed.
A packet that drifts from the underlying tables must be regenerated on rule/evidence correction.

options.name: Event-log replay
options.summary: Keep only the append-only event log and rebuild the packet on demand from events.
pros: Single source of truth; no packet to keep in sync.
Cheap at write time; only appealed cases pay the reconstruction cost.
cons: Replay logic must be versioned so an old case reconstructs under the rules it actually used.
Slower and riskier at appeal time if the event schema has since evolved.

concepts.definition: A determination record structured so the affected person and an independent reviewer can understand and re-examine the decision.
concepts.whyItMatters: Contestability requires a record legible to the applicant, not just an internal trace for auditors.
concepts.example: The appeal packet shows every rule, figure, and source the decision rested on, in plain language.

whyNow: A legible record and a real appeal path are what make the decision genuinely contestable.

deepDives.title: Independence and partitioning
points: The appeal reviewer must be independent of the original decider.
Application free-text is untrusted and partitioned from the rule engine and record.
PII is scoped and access is logged for accountability.

deepDives.title: Deadlines, notice, and record export
points: Notice delivery starts the statutory appeal clock; the appeal deadline is computed from delivery, not from the decision, and tracked per case.
The reviewer disposition is an explicit state — upheld, reversed, or remanded — written back to the record and audited.
An appeal packet can be exported on demand: rules applied (with versions), evidence used, the explanation, and the full status history, so a reviewer can re-decide from the record alone.
Notices honor the applicant's language and accessibility needs.

recap.before: Records served compliance, not the applicant.
recap.after: The record is legible and an appeal path routes it to an independent reviewer.
recap.newRisk: Decisions must also be consistent across applicants and groups, not just individually correct.

steps.title: 6. Bias-at-Scale: Consistency Across the Population
description: Individual contestability isn't enough — the system must be fair across the whole population. A fairness monitor compares matched cohorts (similar applicants with similar evidence), not just raw approval rates, and watches for caseworker-level outliers, surfacing systematic disparity for review.

The primary mechanism is statistical and policy review, not an LLM verdict: an LLM-as-judge can support explanation-quality checks, but disparate-impact analysis is statistical. This is the population-scale analogue of the HR vertical's adverse-impact monitoring, applied to benefits decisions where systematic inconsistency is itself an injustice.

view.caption: A fairness monitor tracks determination rates and consistency across groups and caseworkers at population scale.

steps.decisionPrompt: How do you detect that similar applicants are getting systematically different outcomes?

options.name: Matched-cohort analysis
options.summary: Compare outcomes for applicants with similar evidence and circumstance, controlling for legitimate factors.
pros: Catches disparate impact that aggregate rates hide (composition differences explain raw gaps).
Localizes disparity to caseworkers, programs, or rule paths for targeted remediation.
cons: Requires governed access to protected attributes and careful cohort construction.
More statistical and operational machinery than a dashboard.

options.name: Aggregate-rate dashboards
options.summary: Track raw approval/denial rates by group over time.
pros: Cheap and easy to read; a useful first alarm.
No need to match individuals or model legitimate factors.
cons: Confounds composition with bias — a rate gap may be lawful or may hide one.
Can't attribute disparity to a decision-maker or rule, so it rarely drives remediation.

concepts.definition: Tracking determination rates and outcomes across groups and decision-makers to surface systematic disparity.
concepts.whyItMatters: A decision can be individually defensible yet part of a biased pattern; only population-scale monitoring catches it.
concepts.example: Two demographically different cohorts with similar evidence show divergent approval rates -> flag for review.

whyNow: Population-scale fairness is a distinct obligation from individual contestability.

deepDives.title: From signal to remediation
points: Cohorts are defined by similar evidence and circumstance, and compared against matched applicants rather than raw approval rates.
Caseworker-level outlier detection flags decision-makers whose adverse rates diverge on comparable cases.

A detected disparity opens a remediation workflow: pull an audit sample, then policy review, model/extraction review, caseworker retraining, rule correction, or retroactive re-review of affected cases.

Population fairness is primarily a statistical, policy, and operational review; an LLM-as-judge is scoped to explanation-quality and contestability checks (is the notice legible and complete?), not to deciding whether disparate impact exists.

deepDives.title: Protected-attribute governance
points: Protected-attribute data lives in a separate analytics partition; only the fairness team holds access, scoped and logged, and solely for disparity analysis.

Demographic attributes are never on the determination path — the rule engine, intake node, and caseworker view never read them, so they can't influence an individual decision or leak into an applicant-facing explanation.

An escalation ladder gives remediation authority teeth: a flagged disparity becomes a case audit sample, then policy review, then rule correction or retroactive re-review, with the threshold for each step defined up front rather than ad hoc.
Fairness findings are audited at the cohort level; reports cite aggregate disparities and remediation actions, never an individual's protected attributes.

recap.before: Fairness was assumed from individual correctness.
recap.after: Matched-cohort consistency is monitored, outliers flagged, and disparities trigger a remediation workflow.
recap.newRisk: Due-process and accountability obligations must be operationalized and the system evaluated.

steps.title: 7. Due-Process Compliance & Evaluation
description: Operate the platform under its obligations: administrative due-process and algorithmic-accountability requirements (notice, explanation, the right to appeal), with every evidence item, rule, explanation, decision, and appeal recorded.

Evaluate beyond accuracy: measure determination correctness, explanation quality, and contestability (could a reviewer actually re-decide from the record?), so an efficient pipeline never hides decisions people can't understand or challenge.

view.caption: Due-process obligations are recorded; eval weights determination correctness, explanation quality, and contestability.

steps.decisionPrompt: Why is determination accuracy alone an insufficient measure of this system's quality?

concepts.definition: Measuring whether the record actually lets an applicant understand and a reviewer re-decide, not just whether the outcome was correct.
concepts.whyItMatters: Due process is about the process, not only the result; a correct-but-opaque decision still fails.
concepts.example: Audit whether reviewers can reach the same decision from the record alone.

whyNow: Process-aware evaluation is what keeps the platform just as it scales.

deepDives.title: Eval artifacts
points: Golden determinations: fixed evidence + rule package with a known-correct outcome, replayed on every rule or model change.
Reviewer replay tests: can an independent reviewer reach the same decision from the exported record alone?
Explanation readability and multilingual/accessibility checks on the applicant-facing notice.

Time-based obligation checks: adverse-pending age against SLA, notice-deadline compliance, correct appeal-window computation from delivery, and reviewer-disposition SLA — the operational deadlines that make 'human in the loop' real rather than failure-by-delay.
Adversarial document-injection tests: a malicious uploaded document must not alter the determination or leak across cases.

recap.after: Correctness, explanation quality, and contestability are evaluated and audited.
recap.newRisk: The last phase-3 vertical adds a physical-experiment gate — see the plan.

finalDesign.title: Final Design — Agentic Public Benefits Platform
finalDesign.description: An application enters through the benefits API. A deterministic determination pipeline has an intake agent extract evidence and draft a plain-language explanation in bounded LLM nodes, then a deterministic rule engine applies the eligibility rule in force for the applicant's date and jurisdiction — the model never decides. Adverse determinations enter an adverse-review queue with priority, SLA aging, retry, and deadline escalation, where a caseworker decides every adverse outcome, with the decision bound to an accountable principal, and each determination ships with the rules applied, evidence used, and reasons needed to contest it. A notice service owns the applicant-facing loop — evidence-needed requests, the legible notice in the applicant's language and accessible format, the appeal clock that starts on delivery, and appeal-packet access. An appeal path routes the legible determination record to an independent reviewer; a fairness monitor tracks consistency across groups and caseworkers at population scale, with protected attributes held in a governed analytics partition off the determination path; and PII is scoped while application text is partitioned from the rule engine. Workflow status and eligibility outcome are tracked as separate axes so the lifecycle is unambiguous. Every evidence item, rule, explanation, decision, and appeal is audited, and evaluation weights determination correctness, explanation quality, contestability, and time-based obligations. Grounding, identity/PII scoping, eval, and observability are the shared substrate from Agentic Platform Foundations; uniquely, the gate produces a contestable decision with due process by construction.

functional.how: Bounded intake node extracts structured evidence against the grounded applicant record.
Deterministic rule engine on effective-dated rules.
Plain-language explanation tied to rules and evidence.
Determination gate bound to an accountable principal.
Record routes to an independent reviewer.

nonFunctional.how: Rule engine the model cannot override.
Consistency monitoring across groups and caseworkers.
Append-only record of evidence, rules, explanation, decision, appeals.

say: Frame the new gate dimension: contestability / due process — the record must be legible to the affected person.
It builds on Agentic Platform Foundations.
Draw the agent deciding opaquely and explain why that denies due process.
Effective-dated, jurisdiction-tagged rules, then a deterministic rule engine the model can't override.
Caseworker decides adverse outcomes; the decision is contestable; audit-for-appeal and the appeal path.
Population-scale fairness, and eval that weights contestability, not just accuracy.

expectations: Agent gathers evidence and produces a determination.
Effective-dated rule grounding and a deterministic rule engine.
Caseworker gate; a legible, appealable record.
Designs audit-for-appeal so a reviewer can re-decide from the record.
Keeps the model out of the decision and adds population-scale consistency monitoring.
Evaluates contestability, not just accuracy; builds on the Foundations substrate.

followUps: What must a determination record contain so an applicant can actually contest it?
How do you keep the rule engine applying the correct version as statutes change?
How do you detect inconsistent outcomes across caseworkers at population scale?
How does the contestability gate differ from the finance and healthcare gates in the series?

links.group: Due Process, Notices, and Public-Benefits Accountability
links.groupDescription: Legal and policy foundations for benefits decisions that affect rights and must be understandable, reviewable, and contestable.
links.title: Goldberg v. Kelly, 397 U.S. 254 (1970)
links.why: Establishes welfare-benefit termination as a due-process problem and motivates notice, reasons, evidence, and an impartial decision-maker instead of an opaque model denial.

links.group: Due Process, Notices, and Public-Benefits Accountability
links.groupDescription: Legal and policy foundations for benefits decisions that affect rights and must be understandable, reviewable, and contestable.
links.title: Mathews v. Eldridge, 424 U.S. 319 (1976)
links.why: Frames procedural safeguards around private interest, error risk, value of additional process, and government burden, which maps directly to the contestability and evaluation design.

links.group: Due Process, Notices, and Public-Benefits Accountability
links.groupDescription: Legal and policy foundations for benefits decisions that affect rights and must be understandable, reviewable, and contestable.
links.title: Government by Algorithm: Artificial Intelligence in Federal Administrative Agencies
links.source: Stanford Law School / Administrative Conference of the United States
links.why: Surveys federal agency AI use and administrative-law risks, grounding the design choice to keep AI assistive and keep adjudicative accountability with governed procedures.

links.group: Due Process, Notices, and Public-Benefits Accountability
links.groupDescription: Legal and policy foundations for benefits decisions that affect rights and must be understandable, reviewable, and contestable.
links.title: M-24-10: Advancing Governance, Innovation, and Risk Management for Agency Use of Artificial Intelligence
links.why: Defines governance and minimum practices for rights-impacting and safety-impacting federal AI, useful for operating the platform under public-sector AI controls.

links.group: Due Process, Notices, and Public-Benefits Accountability
links.groupDescription: Legal and policy foundations for benefits decisions that affect rights and must be understandable, reviewable, and contestable.
links.title: IT Accessibility Laws and Policies
links.why: Connects applicant notices, appeal packets, and public-facing records to federal accessibility obligations so contestability works for disabled applicants too.

links.group: Public Benefits Delivery and Rules as Code
links.groupDescription: Production-adjacent examples and rule-engine references for benefits intake, eligibility logic, and policy-governed automation.
links.title: Social Safety Net
links.why: Shows real public-benefits modernization patterns: integrated applications, ex parte renewals, outreach reminders, caseworker tooling, and constrained AI-assisted operations.

links.group: Public Benefits Delivery and Rules as Code
links.groupDescription: Production-adjacent examples and rule-engine references for benefits intake, eligibility logic, and policy-governed automation.
links.title: OpenFisca Documentation
links.why: Documents an open-source engine for writing tax and benefit rules as code, including legal-to-code modeling, tests, web APIs, and limits around human judgment.

links.group: Public Benefits Delivery and Rules as Code
links.groupDescription: Production-adjacent examples and rule-engine references for benefits intake, eligibility logic, and policy-governed automation.
links.title: OpenFisca Core
links.why: Provides a concrete rules-engine implementation and package model to compare against custom eligibility engines and country or jurisdiction rule packages.

links.group: Public Benefits Delivery and Rules as Code
links.groupDescription: Production-adjacent examples and rule-engine references for benefits intake, eligibility logic, and policy-governed automation.
links.title: Decision Model and Notation (DMN)
links.why: Defines decision tables and executable decision logic that policy, legal, and engineering reviewers can inspect together for deterministic eligibility rules.

links.group: Public Benefits Delivery and Rules as Code
links.groupDescription: Production-adjacent examples and rule-engine references for benefits intake, eligibility logic, and policy-governed automation.
links.title: Open Policy Agent Documentation
links.why: Useful for designing policy-as-code controls around scoped evidence access, delegated caseworker authority, and service-to-service enforcement boundaries.

links.groupDescription: Workflow and security references for adverse-review queues, evidence waits, appeal clocks, and audit-for-appeal records.
links.title: Temporal Workflow
links.why: Explains durable workflow execution, event history, deterministic replay, and long-running processes that fit evidence waits, SLA aging, and appeal deadlines.

links.groupDescription: Workflow and security references for adverse-review queues, evidence waits, appeal clocks, and audit-for-appeal records.
links.title: Discover service integration patterns in Step Functions
links.why: Shows callback-token workflow patterns that map to human approval, external reviewer callbacks, and asynchronous caseworker gates.

links.groupDescription: Workflow and security references for adverse-review queues, evidence waits, appeal clocks, and audit-for-appeal records.
links.title: LLM01:2025 Prompt Injection
links.why: Details direct and indirect prompt-injection risks and mitigations such as least privilege, segregating external content, adversarial testing, and human approval for high-risk actions.

links.groupDescription: Operational references for measuring bias, governance quality, and contestability beyond raw model or rule accuracy.
links.title: AI Risk Management Framework
links.why: Provides a lifecycle risk-management structure for governance, mapping, measuring, and managing AI risks in a rights-affecting public platform.

links.groupDescription: Operational references for measuring bias, governance quality, and contestability beyond raw model or rule accuracy.
links.title: Towards a Standard for Identifying and Managing Bias in Artificial Intelligence
links.why: Frames bias as a lifecycle and sociotechnical risk, supporting protected-attribute governance and matched-cohort review instead of raw approval-rate dashboards.

links.groupDescription: Operational references for measuring bias, governance quality, and contestability beyond raw model or rule accuracy.
links.title: The Bias and Fairness Audit Toolkit for Machine Learning
links.source: Center for Data Science and Public Policy
links.why: Provides audit metrics, disparity views, and subgroup analysis patterns that fit population-scale consistency monitoring across applicants and caseworkers.

links.groupDescription: Operational references for measuring bias, governance quality, and contestability beyond raw model or rule accuracy.
links.title: AI Fairness 360 Documentation
links.why: Offers fairness metrics, bias detection, and mitigation algorithms to compare with the platform's fairness monitor and cohort-analysis pipeline.
